---
title: "Linear Regression & Classification"
subtitle: "Week 2 — Predicting Numbers and Categories (ISLP Ch. 3–4)"
author: "Ho-min Park"
date: 2026-07-17
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.08
    slide-number: c/t
    center: true
    transition: fade
    footer: "ML Summer Class · Week 2"
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    html-math-method: katex
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.1/css/all.min.css">
          <style>
          /* Added for Week 2: vote slides, sighting badge, threshold widget.
             All new styling lives here, so custom.scss stays untouched.
             Retune the deck's identity in one place: */
          :root {
            --w2-navy:  #1b3a5c;   /* deck headings / dividers */
            --w2-amber: #e0a33e;   /* deck rule */
            --w2-no:    #56b4e9;   /* "No default"  — same blue as the ISL figures */
            --w2-yes:   #e8873a;   /* "Default"     — same orange as the ISL figures */
            --w2-alarm: #c0392b;   /* the number that should hurt */
            --w2-mute:  #7c8a99;
          }

          /* Vote slides: one question, nothing else to read. */
          .reveal .vote {
            text-align: center;
            font-size: 1.5em;
            line-height: 1.35;
            max-width: 22em;
            margin: 0 auto;
          }
          .reveal .vote .opts {
            display: flex; gap: 1.2em; justify-content: center;
            margin-top: 1.1em; font-size: .72em; color: var(--w2-mute);
          }
          .reveal .vote .opts b { color: var(--w2-navy); }
          .reveal .hands {
            margin-top: 1.4em; font-size: .68em; letter-spacing: .06em;
            text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--w2-amber); font-weight: 700;
          }

          /* The running scorecard. Three sightings, one lecture. */
          .reveal .sighting {
            display: inline-block; margin-top: .5em;
            border-left: 5px solid var(--w2-amber);
            padding: .18em .7em; font-weight: 700; color: var(--w2-navy);
          }

          /* The cascade on the accuracy-trap slide. */
          .reveal .cascade { font-size: 1.15em; line-height: 1.9; }
          .reveal .cascade .num { font-weight: 700; color: var(--w2-navy); }
          .reveal .cascade .bad { font-weight: 700; color: var(--w2-alarm); }
          .reveal .punch {
            font-size: 1.5em; font-weight: 700; color: var(--w2-alarm);
            line-height: 1.3;
          }

          /* ---- threshold widget ----
             Every selector is prefixed with .reveal: reveal.js styles
             `.reveal table th/td` directly, and would otherwise outrank us. */
          .reveal .tw { font-size: .8em; }
          .reveal .tw-control {
            display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 1em;
            margin: 0 0 .9em; font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums;
          }
          .reveal .tw-control label {
            font-weight: 700; color: var(--w2-navy); white-space: nowrap;
          }
          .reveal .tw-control input[type=range] {
            flex: 1; height: 10px; accent-color: var(--w2-amber); cursor: pointer;
          }
          .reveal .tw-control input[type=range]:focus-visible {
            outline: 3px solid var(--w2-navy); outline-offset: 4px;
          }
          .reveal .tw-control output {
            min-width: 2.6em; font-weight: 700; font-size: 1.15em;
            color: var(--w2-amber);
          }

          .reveal .tw-body { display: flex; gap: 2.2em; align-items: center; }

          .reveal table.tw-cm {
            margin: 0; border-collapse: collapse;
            font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums;
          }
          .reveal table.tw-cm th,
          .reveal table.tw-cm td {
            border: 1px solid #d6dde4; padding: .34em .8em; text-align: center;
          }
          .reveal table.tw-cm thead th {
            font-size: .78em; font-weight: 400;
            color: var(--w2-mute); border: 0;
          }
          .reveal table.tw-cm tbody th {
            font-size: .8em; text-align: right; border: 0;
          }
          .reveal table.tw-cm td {
            font-size: 1.15em; font-weight: 700; color: var(--w2-navy);
          }
          .reveal table.tw-cm .tw-h-no  { color: var(--w2-no); }
          .reveal table.tw-cm .tw-h-yes { color: var(--w2-yes); }
          .reveal table.tw-cm td.tw-miss {
            color: var(--w2-alarm); background: #fdf0ee;
          }
          .reveal table.tw-cm td.tw-hit { background: #fdf3e8; }

          .reveal .tw-readouts { flex: 1; }
          .reveal .tw-big { line-height: 1; }
          .reveal .tw-big span {
            font-size: 3.2em; font-weight: 800; color: var(--w2-alarm);
          }
          .reveal .tw-big .tw-unit { font-size: 1.4em; }
          .reveal .tw-row { display: flex; gap: 2.4em; margin-top: 1em; }
          .reveal .tw-row span {
            font-size: 1.7em; font-weight: 700; color: var(--w2-navy);
          }
          .reveal .tw-row .tw-unit { font-size: 1em; }
          .reveal .tw-lab {
            font-size: .62em; letter-spacing: .04em; text-transform: uppercase;
            color: var(--w2-mute); margin-top: .35em;
          }
          .reveal .tw-null {
            margin-top: 1.4em; padding-top: .7em; border-top: 1px solid #d6dde4;
            font-size: .72em; color: var(--w2-mute);
          }
          .reveal .tw-null b { color: var(--w2-navy); }
          </style>
---

## Last week, in one line

Everything we do is estimating the $f$ in $Y = f(X) + \epsilon$ — and what
matters is [test error, not training error]{.hl}.

::: notes
One line is enough this week. Flag the split for today: Chapter 3 predicts a
number, Chapter 4 predicts a category. Same machinery, different output.
:::

## Today's data: what is X, what is Y

| Dataset | X (input · predictors) | Y (output · response) | Y is a… |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Advertising` | TV / radio / newspaper spend | `sales` | number → **regression** |
| `Auto` | `horsepower` | `mpg` | number → **regression** |
| `Default` | `balance`, `income`, `student` | `default` (Yes/No) | category → **classification** |
| `Boston` *(lab)* | `lstat`, `age`, … | `medv` (median value) | number → **regression** |
| `Smarket` *(lab)* | last 5 days' returns, volume | `Direction` (Up/Down) | category → **classification** |

- **X is what we already know** (and can sometimes control); **Y is what we
  want to know**
- The only fork in today's road: [Y is a number → Chapter 3; Y is a category
  → Chapter 4]{.hl}. The machinery barely changes.

::: notes
Row three, `Default`, is the dataset Chapter 4 runs on. Every row on this table
appears within the next ninety minutes; treat it as the cast of characters,
thirty seconds per row, no more.
:::

# Chapter 3 · Regression<br>Predict a Number {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Chapter 3 — the map {.smaller}

One dataset, `Advertising`, carries the whole chapter:

- **The model** — fit a line by least squares; read $\beta_0, \beta_1$ as
  statements about the business
- **Two spaces** — one line in data space = one point in parameter space;
  [fitting = finding the lowest point]{.hl}
- **Scoring** — $R^2$ measures fit, but only context makes it *good*
- **More predictors** — `sales ~ TV + radio`: each coefficient holds the
  others fixed
- **Beyond lines** — polynomials on `Auto`: more flexible fits always look
  better on training data
- **Lab** — you refit every number yourself in `week2-lab`, Part A

::: notes
Thirty seconds — this is a map, not content. Every bullet becomes 2–4 slides.
:::

## The simplest useful model

Assume the relationship between $X$ and $Y$ is (approximately) a straight line:

$$Y \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$$

For the `Advertising` data: $\texttt{sales} \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 \times \texttt{TV}$.

::: {.fragment}
- A **parametric** method in its purest form: estimating $f$ reduces to
  estimating just two numbers
- Dull? Maybe. But [it is often surprisingly hard to beat]{.hl} — and by the
  end of this hour you will watch it beat a method that assumes nothing at all
:::

::: notes
Plant the seed for the KNN reveal here. They should be slightly suspicious that
the boring model is being set up to win something.
:::

## Meet the data: `Advertising` {.dataset .smaller}

200 markets. Does spending on TV, radio, or newspaper ads move product sales?
The running example for all of Chapter 3.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:46%;"}
**Inputs (X):** TV · Radio · Newspaper ad budgets, in \$1000s

**Output (Y):** Sales, in 1000s of units → a **number → regression**

*n = 200 markets · p = 3 predictors*
:::
::: {.column style="width:54%;"}
| TV | Radio | Newspaper | Sales |
|--:|--:|--:|--:|
| 230.1 | 37.8 | 69.2 | 22.1 |
| 44.5 | 39.3 | 45.1 | 10.4 |
| 17.2 | 45.9 | 69.3 | 9.3 |
| 151.5 | 41.3 | 58.5 | 18.5 |
:::
::::

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
try:
    from ISLP import load_data
    adv = load_data("Advertising")
except Exception:
    try:
        adv = pd.read_csv("data/Advertising.csv", index_col=0)
    except Exception:
        adv = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nguyen-toan/"
                          "ISLR/master/dataset/Advertising.csv", index_col=0)
NAVY, AMBER, ALARM, GRAY = "#1b3a5c", "#e0a33e", "#c0392b", "#8a97a5"
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13.2, 3.5), dpi=150,
    gridspec_kw=dict(wspace=0.26, left=0.05, right=0.99, top=0.88, bottom=0.17))
for ax, (col, c) in zip(axes, [("TV", NAVY), ("Radio", AMBER), ("Newspaper", GRAY)]):
    x, y = adv[col].values, adv["Sales"].values
    ax.scatter(x, y, s=14, color=c, alpha=0.55, linewidths=0)
    b1, b0 = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)
    xs = np.array([x.min(), x.max()])
    ax.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color=ALARM, lw=2)
    ax.set_title(f"Sales vs {col}", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", color=NAVY)
    ax.set_xlabel(col + "  ($1000s)", fontsize=11.5)
    if col == "TV":
        ax.set_ylabel("Sales (1000s)", fontsize=11.5)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=9)
    for s in ("top", "right"):
        ax.spines[s].set_visible(False)
plt.show()
```

::: notes
Thirty seconds. The three panels are the whole reason multiple regression lands
later — students should leave this slide having *seen* that all three predictors
correlate with sales on their own.
:::

## Least squares: how the line is chosen

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig3-1-least-squares.png" alt=""></div>
```

Each grey segment is a **residual** $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$. Least squares picks
the intercept and slope that minimize the **residual sum of squares**
$\text{RSS} = e_1^2 + e_2^2 + \cdots + e_n^2$.

## Two spaces: one line = one point

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import ConnectionPatch

NAVY, AMBER, ALARM = "#1b3a5c", "#e0a33e", "#c0392b"
GRAY, LINEGRAY = "#8a97a5", "#c3ccd4"

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
n = 100
x = rng.uniform(5, 295, n)
y = np.clip(7 + 0.048 * x + rng.normal(0, 3.2, n), 0.5, None)
b0h, b1h = np.linalg.lstsq(np.column_stack([np.ones(n), x]), y, rcond=None)[0]

cands = [("A", 15.0, 0.008, ALARM, "--"),
         ("B", 1.0, 0.082, AMBER, "--"),
         ("C", b0h, b1h, NAVY, "-")]

B0 = np.linspace(-2, 19, 260)
B1 = np.linspace(-0.006, 0.104, 260)
G0, G1 = np.meshgrid(B0, B1)
Z = ((y[None, None, :] - (G0[..., None] + G1[..., None] * x[None, None, :])) ** 2).sum(-1)

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(
    1, 2, figsize=(13.2, 4.75), dpi=150,
    gridspec_kw=dict(wspace=0.30, left=0.055, right=0.985, top=0.88, bottom=0.145))

ax1.scatter(x, y, s=17, color=GRAY, alpha=0.55, linewidths=0, zorder=2)
xx = np.array([0, 300])
for name, b0, b1, col, ls in cands:
    ax1.plot(xx, b0 + b1 * xx, ls, color=col, lw=2.6 if name == "C" else 2.0, zorder=3)
    ax1.text(252, b0 + b1 * 252 + 1.15, name, color=col, fontsize=14,
             fontweight="bold", ha="center", va="bottom", zorder=4,
             bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.15", fc="white", ec="none", alpha=0.85))
ax1.set_xlim(0, 300)
ax1.set_ylim(0, 30)
ax1.set_xlabel("TV", fontsize=13)
ax1.set_ylabel("Sales", fontsize=13)
ax1.set_title("Data space", fontsize=15.5, fontweight="bold", color=NAVY, pad=9)
ax1.tick_params(labelsize=10.5)
for s in ("top", "right"):
    ax1.spines[s].set_visible(False)

levels = Z.min() * np.array([1.05, 1.2, 1.5, 2.2, 3.5, 6, 10, 16, 25])
cs = ax2.contour(G0, G1, Z, levels=levels, colors=[LINEGRAY], linewidths=1.1, zorder=1)
ax2.contour(G0, G1, Z, levels=levels[:2], colors=[AMBER], linewidths=1.4, zorder=2)
ax2.clabel(cs, levels=[levels[3], levels[6]], fmt=lambda v: f"RSS {v:,.0f}",
           fontsize=8.5, colors="#93a1ad")
for name, b0, b1, col, _ in cands:
    if name == "C":
        ax2.scatter([b0], [b1], marker="*", s=340, color=col, zorder=5,
                    edgecolors="white", linewidths=0.8)
        dx, dy = 1.1, 0.0035
    else:
        ax2.scatter([b0], [b1], s=72, color=col, zorder=5,
                    edgecolors="white", linewidths=0.8)
        dx, dy = 0.9, 0.003
    ax2.text(b0 + dx, b1 + dy, name, color=col, fontsize=14, fontweight="bold",
             ha="left", va="center", zorder=6)
ax2.set_xlim(B0[0], B0[-1])
ax2.set_ylim(B1[0], B1[-1])
ax2.set_xlabel(r"$\beta_0$  (intercept)", fontsize=13)
ax2.set_ylabel(r"$\beta_1$  (slope)", fontsize=13)
ax2.set_title("Parameter space", fontsize=15.5, fontweight="bold", color=NAVY, pad=9)
ax2.tick_params(labelsize=10.5)
for s in ("top", "right"):
    ax2.spines[s].set_visible(False)

con = ConnectionPatch(
    xyA=(260.0, b0h + b1h * 260.0), coordsA=ax1.transData,
    xyB=(b0h, b1h), coordsB=ax2.transData,
    arrowstyle="-|>", mutation_scale=13,
    color=NAVY, lw=1.3, linestyle=(0, (2, 3)), alpha=0.65, zorder=1)
fig.add_artist(con)
fig.text(0.505, 0.585, "one line  =  one point", ha="center", fontsize=11.5,
         color=NAVY, fontweight="bold",
         bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.32", fc="#fdf6e8", ec=AMBER, lw=1))
plt.show()
```

**Left (data space)** — axes are $X$ and $Y$: a dot is one observation, a line
is one candidate model. **Right (parameter space)** — axes are $\beta_0$ and
$\beta_1$: [a point is an entire line from the left panel]{.hl}; the contours
are RSS.

Lines A · B · C *are* points A · B · C — least squares C sits at the bottom of
the bowl. Fitting = finding the lowest point on the right; [Week 5's neural
networks descend a surface like this by gradient descent]{.hl}.

Read the axes first: $X, Y$ → data space; $\beta$'s → parameter space.

::: notes
Ninety percent of later confusion — loss landscapes, gradient descent, "why
doesn't the loss curve look like the regression curve" — comes from putting
the line and the point in the same space. Spend a full minute here. Trace
line C to point C with the laser. This extends the textbook's Figure 3.2,
which shows only the right-hand panel.
:::

## Our line is an estimate — from one sample

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig3-3-population-vs-ls.png" alt=""></div>
```

Red: the true *population* line. Dark blue: the least squares line from one
sample. Light blue: least squares lines from ten other samples. Each line
differs, but on average they land on the truth — [each sample gives a
slightly different line]{.hl}.

## More than one predictor

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_p X_p + \epsilon$$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:45%;"}
- Each $\beta_j$: the average effect on $Y$ of a one-unit increase in $X_j$,
  [holding all other predictors fixed]{.hl}
- With two predictors, the fitted "line" becomes a plane; beyond that,
  a hyperplane
:::
::: {.column style="width:55%;"}
```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig3-4-regression-plane.png" alt=""></div>
```
:::
::::

::: notes
Lean hard on "holding all other predictors fixed." It is the whole reason the
next slide is surprising.
:::

## Two predictors in action: `sales ~ TV + radio` {.dataset .smaller}

Fit the plane by least squares on the real `Advertising` data:

$$\widehat{\texttt{sales}} = 2.92 + 0.046\,\texttt{TV} + 0.188\,\texttt{radio}$$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:42%;"}
- The plane is the 2-predictor version of the line — each red stem is a
  **residual**, and least squares tilts the plane to minimise their squares
- Read a coefficient the right way: **+0.188** is the effect of one more \$1k
  of radio *at a fixed TV budget*
- Two predictors together explain [$R^2 = 0.90$]{.hl}, well above TV alone
  ($R^2 = 0.61$)
:::
::: {.column style="width:58%;"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa
try:
    from ISLP import load_data
    adv = load_data("Advertising")
except Exception:
    try:
        adv = pd.read_csv("data/Advertising.csv", index_col=0)
    except Exception:
        adv = pd.read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nguyen-toan/"
                          "ISLR/master/dataset/Advertising.csv", index_col=0)
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(adv)), adv.TV, adv.Radio])
y = adv.Sales.values
b, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)
NAVY, ALARM = "#1b3a5c", "#c0392b"
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(7.4, 5.4), dpi=150)
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
tv = np.linspace(adv.TV.min(), adv.TV.max(), 20)
ra = np.linspace(adv.Radio.min(), adv.Radio.max(), 20)
TV, RA = np.meshgrid(tv, ra)
ax.plot_surface(TV, RA, b[0] + b[1] * TV + b[2] * RA, alpha=0.35,
                color="#7fb3d5", edgecolor="none")
yhat = X @ b
for i in range(len(adv)):
    ax.plot([adv.TV.iloc[i]] * 2, [adv.Radio.iloc[i]] * 2,
            [y[i], yhat[i]], color=ALARM, lw=0.4, alpha=0.4)
ax.scatter(adv.TV, adv.Radio, y, s=13, color=NAVY, alpha=0.65)
ax.set_xlabel("TV", fontsize=11, labelpad=1)
ax.set_ylabel("radio", fontsize=11, labelpad=1)
ax.set_zlabel("Sales", fontsize=11, labelpad=6)
ax.view_init(elev=18, azim=-58)
ax.tick_params(labelsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::
::::

::: notes
This is the concrete instance of the previous slide's abstract plane (ISLR
Fig 3.5). Point at one red stem — that residual is exactly the $e_i$ from the
least-squares slide, now in 3D. The coefficient reading ("at a fixed TV
budget") is the whole point of multiple regression — each slope is measured with
the other predictors held fixed.
:::

## $R^2 = 0.61$. Is that good? {.center}

::: {.vote}
The TV model explains **61%** of the variability in sales.

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{RSS}}{\text{TSS}}$$

::: {.opts}
<b>Good</b> · <b>Bad</b> · <b>Cannot tell</b>
:::

::: {.hands}
Hands up
:::
:::

::: notes
Let them commit before clicking. Most rooms split, which is the point.
:::

## Cannot tell — and that is the honest answer

$R^2$ has no universal scale. **Physics expects 0.99. Social science
celebrates 0.3.** The same 0.61 is a triumph in one field and a red flag in
another.

::: {.fragment}
The honest move is to translate the miss into units the decision-maker cares
about: look back at the gray residual segments — on a typical market the line
misses by [a few thousand units of sales]{.hl}. Now go ask a marketing manager
whether that is acceptable.
:::

::: {.fragment}
[A goodness-of-fit number is a question, not an answer.]{.hl} This will happen
again in twenty minutes, with a much more dangerous number.
:::

::: notes
That last line is a deliberate trailer for the accuracy slide in Chapter 4.
Say it lightly; do not spoil it.
:::

## Meet the data: `Auto` {.dataset .smaller}

392 cars. How does an engine's power relate to its fuel economy?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:42%;"}
**Inputs (X):** all 7 remaining columns — cylinders, displacement,
horsepower, weight, acceleration, year, origin

**Output (Y):** mpg (miles per gallon) → a **number → regression**

*n = 392 cars · p = 7 predictors*

| mpg | hp | weight | cyl |
|--:|--:|--:|--:|
| 18.0 | 130 | 3504 | 8 |
| 15.0 | 165 | 3693 | 8 |
| 18.0 | 150 | 3436 | 8 |
| 16.0 | 150 | 3433 | 8 |

*(mini-table previews 4 of the 8 columns)*
:::
::: {.column style="width:58%;"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from ISLP import load_data
auto = load_data("Auto")
NAVY, GRAY = "#1b3a5c", "#8a97a5"
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.7, 4.7), dpi=150)
ax.scatter(auto.horsepower, auto.mpg, s=20, color=GRAY, alpha=0.6, linewidths=0)
ax.set_xlabel("horsepower", fontsize=13)
ax.set_ylabel("mpg", fontsize=13)
ax.set_title("mpg vs horsepower — a curve, not a line",
             fontsize=13.5, fontweight="bold", color=NAVY)
ax.tick_params(labelsize=11)
for s in ("top", "right"):
    ax.spines[s].set_visible(False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::
::::

::: notes
The bend is the point — hold it in their eye for a beat, then the next slide
asks whether degree 2 beats a straight line here. Don't fit anything yet.
:::

## Same model, higher powers {.smaller}

To bend the line, just add powers of `horsepower` as new columns of $X$:

$$\text{degree 1:}\quad \texttt{mpg} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\texttt{hp}$$
$$\text{degree 2:}\quad \texttt{mpg} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\texttt{hp} + \beta_2\,\texttt{hp}^2$$
$$\text{degree 5:}\quad \texttt{mpg} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\texttt{hp} + \beta_2\,\texttt{hp}^2 + \cdots + \beta_5\,\texttt{hp}^5$$

- Every one of these is [still linear regression]{.hl} — linear in the
  coefficients $\beta$. We are not fitting a curved model, we are fitting a
  straight one to extra columns
- Higher degree = more flexibility — the fit hugs the training data more
  tightly. The next slide shows what each degree actually does to the fit
- `I(horsepower**2)` in a formula is exactly this — one more column, nothing
  more exotic

::: notes
This is the slide that makes "adding horsepower² is still linear regression"
click. Stress: linear in β, not in X. Then the plot slide shows degree 2 helping
and degree 5 chasing noise — the equations make the trade-off concrete before
they see it.
:::

## Straight lines are not sacred

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig3-8-auto-polynomial.png" alt=""></div>
```

Degree 2 improves on linear. **Degree 5 starts chasing noise** — look at the
right edge. Adding $\texttt{horsepower}^2$ is *still linear regression*, by the
way: linear in the **coefficients**, not in $X$.


## This week's lab, part 1: `week2-lab`, Part A

One file, zero setup: open **`week2-lab.ipynb`** in Jupyter or Colab and
**Run All**. The first cell installs anything missing, and the `Advertising`
data is embedded in the notebook itself — [nothing to download, nothing to
configure]{.hl}.

```python
ADVERTISING = {"TV": [230.1, 44.5, ...], "Radio": [...], "Sales": [...]}
adv = pd.DataFrame(ADVERTISING)              # dict of lists -> a table
m1  = smf.ols("Sales ~ TV", data=adv).fit()  # today's line, refit by you
```

- **A0–A1** — build the DataFrame from raw Python data, refit today's line
- **A3–A4** — recompute $R^2 = 0.61 \to 0.90$ with your own residuals
- Every headline number from these slides is reproduced in the notebook
- Going deeper (interactions, qualitative predictors): official ISLP lab 3.6

::: notes
Put the course-repo download link on this slide before class. If there is
time, demo Run All live — the point is that it works on a blank machine.
:::

## Regression, in three lines {.center}

::: {.vote style="max-width:1180px;"}
**1.** Fit a line or plane by least squares (minimise RSS). A coefficient
$\beta_j$ is the effect of $X_j$ [holding the other predictors fixed]{.hl}.

**2.** $R^2$ says how much variation the model explains — but [only context
says whether that is good]{.hl}. Translate misses into units people care about.

**3.** Straight lines are not sacred: powers of $X$ are still linear
regression, and they always fit the training data better. [Whether they
*predict* better is next week's question.]{.hl}
:::

::: notes
Thirty seconds, said slowly. This is the anchor students should carry into the
lab and into next week. Do not add anything — three lines, then move on.
:::

# Chapter 4 · Classification<br>Predict a Category {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Chapter 4 — the map {.smaller}

One dataset again — `Default`, 10,000 credit-card customers:

- **The problem** — the output is a category (Yes/No), so we model a
  **probability**
- **The S-curve** — logistic regression keeps probabilities inside $(0, 1)$
- **Fitting** — the same two-space picture as Chapter 3, with a new surface
- **Reading results** — the confusion matrix, and why [97% accuracy can
  lie]{.hl}
- **The threshold** — 0.3 vs 0.5 vs 0.7 flag very different customers;
  ROC / AUC compare them all
- **Lab** — `week2-lab`, Parts B–C; your threshold decision is homework #3

::: notes
Same rhythm as the Chapter 3 map. Land on the threshold bullet — it is the
week's real deliverable.
:::

## Meet the data: `Default` {.dataset .smaller}

10,000 credit-card customers. Will this person miss a payment? The running
example for all of Chapter 4.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:42%;"}
**Inputs (X):** balance, income, student (Yes/No)

**Output (Y):** default — Yes/No → a **category → classification**

*n = 10,000 · p = 3 · only 3.33% default*

| default | student | balance | income |
|:--|:--|--:|--:|
| No | No | 730 | 44,362 |
| No | Yes | 817 | 12,106 |
| Yes | Yes | 1,487 | 17,854 |
| Yes | Yes | 2,206 | 14,271 |
:::
::: {.column style="width:58%;"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from ISLP import load_data
d = load_data("Default")
NO, YES = "#3a86c7", "#e8873a"
no = d[d.default == "No"].sample(2000, random_state=1)
ys = d[d.default == "Yes"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.7, 4.7), dpi=150)
ax.scatter(no.balance, no.income, s=10, color=NO, alpha=0.35,
           linewidths=0, label="No default")
ax.scatter(ys.balance, ys.income, s=17, color=YES, alpha=0.8,
           marker="+", label="Default")
ax.set_xlabel("balance", fontsize=13)
ax.set_ylabel("income", fontsize=13)
ax.set_title("balance separates; income does not",
             fontsize=13.5, fontweight="bold", color="#1b3a5c")
ax.legend(fontsize=11, markerscale=1.4, frameon=False)
ax.tick_params(labelsize=11)
for s in ("top", "right"):
    ax.spines[s].set_visible(False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::
::::

::: notes
The orange points pile up at high balance regardless of income — that visual is
what makes logistic regression on balance the obvious next move.
:::

## New problem: predicting a category

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig4-1-default-data.png" alt=""></div>
```

The `Default` data: will a customer **default** on their credit card debt?
Balance clearly separates the orange (default) from blue (no default);
income barely matters. The response is now [Yes/No — not a number]{.hl}.

::: notes
Balance is the workhorse predictor here; income barely matters. Foreshadow that a
straight line is the wrong tool for a Yes/No response — the next slide shows why.
:::

## Why not just run linear regression?

```{=html}
<div class="fig-wrap"><img src="images/fig4-2-linear-vs-logistic.png" alt=""></div>
```

Code No = 0, Yes = 1 and regress? The line (left) produces probabilities
**below zero** and, for other data, above one. Logistic regression (right)
squeezes the output into $(0, 1)$ — a proper probability.

## The logistic model

$$p(X) = \frac{e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X}}{1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X}}$$

::: {.fragment}
Rearranged, the **log odds** are linear in $X$:

$$\log\left(\frac{p(X)}{1 - p(X)}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$$

One more unit of $X$ adds $\beta_1$ to the log odds — the probability itself
moves by a *varying* amount. That is the S-curve.
:::

::: {.fragment}
For $K > 2$ classes, the **softmax** generalisation treats every class
symmetrically — and it is the exact function sitting at the output layer of
every classification network you will meet in [Week 5]{.hl}.
:::

## Fitting: the two-space picture again

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from ISLP import load_data
NAVY, AMBER, ALARM, GRAY = "#1b3a5c", "#e0a33e", "#c0392b", "#8a97a5"
d = load_data("Default")
x = d.balance.to_numpy(); y = (d.default == "Yes").to_numpy().astype(float)

def dev(b0, b1):
    z = np.clip(b0 + b1 * x, -30, 30)
    p = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))
    p = np.clip(p, 1e-12, 1 - 1e-12)
    return -2 * (y * np.log(p) + (1 - y) * np.log(1 - p)).sum()

cands = [("A", -4.0, 0.0016, ALARM, "--"),
         ("B", -16.0, 0.0090, AMBER, "--"),
         ("C", -10.65, 0.0055, NAVY, "-")]

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13.2, 4.6), dpi=150,
    gridspec_kw=dict(wspace=0.28, left=0.055, right=0.985, top=0.87, bottom=0.15))
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
sub = rng.choice(len(x), 1600, replace=False)
ax1.scatter(x[sub], y[sub] + rng.normal(0, 0.012, len(sub)), s=6,
            color=GRAY, alpha=0.35, linewidths=0)
g = np.linspace(0, x.max(), 300)
for name, b0, b1, col, ls in cands:
    ax1.plot(g, 1/(1+np.exp(-(b0 + b1*g))), ls, color=col,
             lw=2.8 if name == "C" else 2.0)
    xi = (0.75 - b0) / b1 if name != "A" else 2300
    ax1.text(min(xi, 2450), 0.80, name, color=col, fontsize=14,
             fontweight="bold",
             bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.15", fc="white", ec="none", alpha=0.85))
ax1.set_xlabel("balance", fontsize=13); ax1.set_ylabel("P(default)", fontsize=13)
ax1.set_title("Data space: each (β₀, β₁) draws one S-curve",
              fontsize=14, fontweight="bold", color=NAVY, pad=9)
B0 = np.linspace(-17.5, -3, 90); B1 = np.linspace(0.0008, 0.0098, 90)
Z = np.array([[dev(b0, b1) for b0 in B0] for b1 in B1])
lv = Z.min() * np.array([1.02, 1.08, 1.2, 1.45, 1.9, 2.6, 3.6, 5.0])
ax2.contour(B0, B1, Z, levels=lv, colors=["#c3ccd4"], linewidths=1.1)
ax2.contour(B0, B1, Z, levels=lv[:2], colors=[AMBER], linewidths=1.4)
for name, b0, b1, col, _ in cands:
    if name == "C":
        ax2.scatter([b0], [b1], marker="*", s=340, color=col, zorder=5,
                    edgecolors="white", linewidths=0.8)
    else:
        ax2.scatter([b0], [b1], s=72, color=col, zorder=5,
                    edgecolors="white", linewidths=0.8)
    ax2.text(b0 + 0.35, b1, name, color=col, fontsize=14, fontweight="bold",
             va="center")
ax2.set_xlabel(r"$\beta_0$", fontsize=13); ax2.set_ylabel(r"$\beta_1$", fontsize=13)
ax2.set_title("Parameter space: the deviance surface",
              fontsize=14, fontweight="bold", color=NAVY, pad=9)
plt.show()
```

**Left:** each $(\beta_0, \beta_1)$ pair draws one S-curve through the data —
[move $\beta_1$ and the curve steepens; move $\beta_0$ and it slides]{.hl}.
**Right:** the same pairs are points on the **deviance** surface (the misfit
score that maximum likelihood minimises). Fitting = walking downhill to C:
$\hat\beta_0 = -10.65$, $\hat\beta_1 = 0.0055$ — so balance \$1,000 →
$\hat{p} = 0.6\%$, \$2,000 → 58.6%.

::: notes
Same picture as the least-squares slide, new surface: the RSS bowl becomes the
deviance bowl. If asked "what is deviance": the likelihood version of RSS —
smaller is better, and the bottom is the fit.
:::

## The confusion matrix {.smaller}

Accuracy compresses everything into one number. The confusion matrix keeps all
four:

| | pred: no | pred: default |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| **truth: no** | **TN** — correctly cleared | **FP** — false alarm |
| **truth: default** | **FN** — [the miss that costs money]{.hl} | **TP** — caught |

- Rows are the truth; columns are what the model said. Every prediction lands
  in exactly one cell, so the four counts always sum to $n$
- The two error types are **not equal**: an FN is a real defaulter we cleared;
  an FP is a good customer we annoyed. [Which is worse is a business
  question, not a statistics question.]{.hl}

::: notes
Concept only — no model numbers yet. The next slide fills the four cells with
our model's actual results.
:::

## Four numbers from the four cells {.smaller}

Every standard metric is a ratio of the same four counts:

| metric | formula | the question it answers |
|:--|:--:|:--|
| **Accuracy** | $(TP + TN)/n$ | how often are we right overall? |
| **Precision** | $TP/(TP + FP)$ | of those we *flagged*, how many were real? |
| **Recall** (= TPR) | $TP/(TP + FN)$ | of the *real* defaulters, how many did we catch? |
| **F1** | $2PR/(P + R)$ | one number balancing precision and recall |

- Accuracy [breaks on imbalanced data]{.hl} — exactly our 3%-default problem
- Precision and recall **fight each other**: raising the threshold trades
  recall for precision — [the threshold fight you are about to watch]{.hl}
- F1 is their harmonic mean: high only when *both* are high

::: notes
Recall is the same number as the TPR three slides ahead — say so now to defuse
the jargon. F1: for when you must report one number on imbalanced classes.
:::

## Your turn: four numbers from these cells {.center}

::: {.vote style="max-width:1080px;"}
A model screened **100 loan applications**:

| | pred: no | pred: default |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| **truth: no** | 50 | 10 |
| **truth: default** | 15 | 25 |

**Accuracy? · Precision? · Recall? · F1?**

::: {.hands}
Work them out — hands up when you have all four
:::
:::

::: notes
Answers (not on the slide): Accuracy 75/100 = 0.75 · Precision 25/35 ≈ 0.71 ·
Recall 25/40 = 0.625 · F1 = 2·(0.714·0.625)/(0.714+0.625) ≈ 0.67.
Take one student per metric. Then ask: "which of the four would the *bank*
care about most, and why?" — that question bridges straight into the next
slide's real model.
:::

## Our model at threshold 0.5 {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:44%;"}
Logistic regression on `Default`; classify as *default* when
$\hat{p} \ge 0.5$:

| | pred: no | pred: default |
|:--|--:|--:|
| **truth: no** | 9,627 | 40 |
| **truth: default** | [**228**]{.hl} | 105 |

- Accuracy **97.3%** — but ["nobody defaults" scores 96.7%]{.hl}
- Missed **228 of 333 defaulters — 68.5%**
- Precision **72.4%** · Recall **31.5%** · F1 **0.44**
:::
::: {.column style="width:56%;"}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
NAVY, AMBER, ALARM, GRAY = "#1b3a5c", "#e0a33e", "#c0392b", "#8a97a5"
b0, b1 = -10.65, 0.0055
g = np.linspace(0, 2700, 400)
p = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(b0 + b1 * g)))
cut = lambda t: (np.log(t / (1 - t)) - b0) / b1
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.9), dpi=150)
ax.plot(g, p, color=NAVY, lw=2.6, zorder=3)
ax.axvspan(cut(0.5), 2700, color="#fbe9e5", zorder=0)
ax.text(2330, 0.06, "flagged at t = 0.5", color=ALARM, fontsize=11, ha="center",
        fontweight="bold")
for t, col, ls, lw in [(0.3, GRAY, "--", 1.5), (0.5, ALARM, "-", 2.0),
                       (0.7, GRAY, "--", 1.5)]:
    c = cut(t)
    ax.axhline(t, color=col, ls=ls, lw=lw, xmax=c / 2700, zorder=2)
    ax.plot([c, c], [0, t], color=col, ls=ls, lw=lw, zorder=2)
    ax.plot([c], [t], "o", ms=7, color=col, mec="white", mew=1.2, zorder=4)
    ax.annotate(f"t = {t}\n${c:,.0f}".replace(",", " "), (c, t),
                textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-64 if t < 0.7 else 10, 10),
                fontsize=10.5, color=col if col != GRAY else "#5f6f7d",
                fontweight="bold")
ax.annotate("", xy=(cut(0.7) + 25, 0.03), xytext=(cut(0.3) - 25, 0.03),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color=AMBER, lw=2))
ax.text((cut(0.3) + cut(0.7)) / 2, 0.085, "moving t slides the cutoff", color="#a8760f",
        fontsize=10.5, ha="center", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlim(0, 2700); ax.set_ylim(-0.02, 1.02)
ax.set_xlabel("balance", fontsize=12); ax.set_ylabel("P(default)", fontsize=12)
for s_ in ("top", "right"):
    ax.spines[s_].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The threshold is a [horizontal line on the S-curve]{.hl}: raise it and the
balance cutoff slides right, flagging fewer people.
:::
::::

::: notes
Curve drawn for the balance-only fit (β₀ = −10.65, β₁ = 0.0055); the counts
come from the full model. t = 0.3 / 0.5 / 0.7 cut at ≈ $1,782 / $1,936 /
$2,090 — the next slide shows what those choices do to the counts.
:::

## Move the threshold: 0.3 vs 0.7 {.smaller}

Same model, same data — only the cut-off changes:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:50%;"}
**$t = 0.3$** — flag anyone above 30%

| | pred: no | pred: default |
|:--|--:|--:|
| **truth: no** | 9,530 | 137 |
| **truth: default** | 164 | 169 |

missed defaulters **49.2%** · overall error **3.0%** · flagged **306**
:::
::: {.column style="width:50%;"}
**$t = 0.7$** — flag only above 70%

| | pred: no | pred: default |
|:--|--:|--:|
| **truth: no** | 9,655 | 12 |
| **truth: default** | 276 | 57 |

missed defaulters **82.9%** · overall error **2.9%** · flagged **69**
:::
::::

Lower the threshold and you catch defaulters but flood the review team; raise
it and the error rate looks fine while defaulters walk through. [The model
cannot pick for you — someone owns this choice.]{.hl}

::: notes
Point at the two "missed" numbers first, then the two "flagged" numbers.
Homework #3 asks each student to commit to a threshold and defend it.
:::

## Two rates summarise any threshold {.smaller}

Every confusion matrix compresses to two rates. At threshold $t$:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:40%;"}
| | pred: no | pred: default |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| **truth: no** | TN | FP |
| **truth: default** | FN | TP |

At $t = 0.5$: TN 9,627 · FP 40 · FN 228 · TP 105.
:::
::: {.column style="width:60%;"}
$$\text{TPR (recall)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = 0.32$$
$$\text{FPR} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = 0.004$$

- **TPR** — of the real defaulters, the share we **catch**
  ($t{=}0.3 \to 0.51$, $t{=}0.7 \to 0.17$)
- **FPR** — of the good customers, the share we **falsely flag**
- Accuracy $= \frac{TP + TN}{n}$ looked great — and [we saw why that lies]{.hl}
  on a 3%-default problem
:::
::::

::: notes
TPR and FPR are the only two numbers the ROC curve needs — every threshold
becomes one (FPR, TPR) point, which is exactly the next slide.
:::

## Predict this one {.center}

::: {.vote}
Right now the threshold is 0.5. Drop it to **0.2** — you flag more customers.

On the ROC plane (FPR →, TPR ↑), the point moves…?

::: {.opts}
<b>up and right</b> · <b>down and left</b> · <b>along the diagonal</b>
:::

::: {.hands}
Commit before I click
:::
:::

## One threshold = one point

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from ISLP import load_data
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve
d = load_data("Default")
X = np.column_stack([d.balance, d.income, (d.student == "Yes").astype(float)])
y = (d.default == "Yes").astype(int).values
p = LogisticRegression(max_iter=5000, C=1e6).fit(X, y).predict_proba(X)[:, 1]
def pt(thr):
    yh = (p >= thr).astype(int)
    tp = ((yh == 1) & (y == 1)).sum(); fp = ((yh == 1) & (y == 0)).sum()
    fn = ((yh == 0) & (y == 1)).sum(); tn = ((yh == 0) & (y == 0)).sum()
    return fp / (fp + tn), tp / (tp + fn)
fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y, p)
NAVY, AMBER = "#1b3a5c", "#e0a33e"
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.9), dpi=150)
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "--", color="#b8c2cc", lw=1.2)
ax.plot(fpr, tpr, color="#c3ccd4", lw=1.5, alpha=0.6)
for thr, c in [(0.5, NAVY), (0.2, AMBER)]:
    fx, ty = pt(thr)
    ax.scatter([fx], [ty], s=130, color=c, zorder=5, edgecolors="white", lw=1.2)
    ax.annotate(f"thr = {thr}\n({fx:.3f}, {ty:.2f})", (fx, ty),
                textcoords="offset points", xytext=(16, -6),
                fontsize=12, color=c, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("False positive rate  =  FP / (FP + TN)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("True positive rate  =  TP / (TP + FN)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(-0.03, 1.03); ax.set_ylim(-0.03, 1.03); ax.tick_params(labelsize=10)
for s in ("top", "right"):
    ax.spines[s].set_visible(False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Each threshold gives one confusion matrix → one $(\text{FPR}, \text{TPR})$
pair → **one point**. Lowering it to 0.2 catches more defaulters (TPR ↑) but
raises false alarms (FPR ↑): [the point climbs up and to the right]{.hl}.

## The ROC curve: all thresholds at once

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from ISLP import load_data
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc
d = load_data("Default")
X = np.column_stack([d.balance, d.income, (d.student == "Yes").astype(float)])
y = (d.default == "Yes").astype(int).values
p = LogisticRegression(max_iter=5000, C=1e6).fit(X, y).predict_proba(X)[:, 1]
fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y, p)
A = auc(fpr, tpr)
def pt(thr):
    yh = (p >= thr).astype(int)
    tp = ((yh == 1) & (y == 1)).sum(); fp = ((yh == 1) & (y == 0)).sum()
    fn = ((yh == 0) & (y == 1)).sum(); tn = ((yh == 0) & (y == 0)).sum()
    return fp / (fp + tn), tp / (tp + fn)
NAVY, ALARM = "#1b3a5c", "#c0392b"
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.9), dpi=150)
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], "--", color="#b8c2cc", lw=1.2, label="random (AUC 0.5)")
ax.fill_between(fpr, tpr, alpha=0.12, color=NAVY)
ax.plot(fpr, tpr, color=NAVY, lw=2.4, zorder=3)
for thr in [0.8, 0.5, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1, 0.05]:
    fx, ty = pt(thr)
    ax.scatter([fx], [ty], s=32, color=ALARM, zorder=4)
ax.text(0.52, 0.34, f"AUC = {A:.3f}", fontsize=16, fontweight="bold", color=NAVY)
ax.set_xlabel("False positive rate", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("True positive rate", fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(-0.03, 1.03); ax.set_ylim(-0.03, 1.03); ax.tick_params(labelsize=10)
ax.legend(fontsize=9, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
for s in ("top", "right"):
    ax.spines[s].set_visible(False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Do that for **every** threshold and the points trace the curve (red dots =
the ones above). The **AUC** is the area under it — a single, threshold-free
score, here [0.95]{.hl}. Read it as: the chance the model scores a random
defaulter above a random non-defaulter. The closer to the top-left corner, the
better.

## This week's lab, part 2: `week2-lab`, Parts B–C

Same notebook, same **Run All**. Part B rebuilds everything you just saw on
`Default` — the 0.5 matrix, the threshold comparison, ROC / AUC — and [cell B5
is homework #3]{.hl}:

```python
THRESHOLD = 0.20      # <- change me, rerun, repeat
# prints your confusion matrix, missed-defaulter %, and error rate
```

Part C points the same machinery at the stock market (`Smarket`) and lands
[barely above a coin flip]{.hl} — not a bug in your code; the most honest
result in the lab. **A model that reports it knows nothing has told you
something true.**

- Optional extras (LDA, QDA, naive Bayes, KNN): official ISLP lab 4.7

::: notes
Same download link as part 1 — one notebook covers both. If time allows, run
cell B5 live with two different thresholds.
:::

## Classification, in three lines {.center}

::: {.vote style="max-width:1180px;"}
**1.** Predict a category by modelling $\Pr(Y \mid X)$: **logistic
regression** squeezes a linear model through the S-curve into $(0, 1)$ — a
proper probability, fitted by walking downhill on the deviance surface.

**2.** One accuracy number can lie. Read the **confusion matrix** — the two
error types cost [different money]{.hl}.

**3.** The **threshold** is a business decision: 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 flag very
different customers. Compare them all at once with **ROC / AUC**.
:::

::: notes
The mirror of the regression recap. Point back to the 0.3-vs-0.7 slide and the
accuracy-trap slide. This is the one-screen summary of the classification half.
:::

# Before Next Friday {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Homework

1. Read **ISLP Ch. 5–6** (`week3/ch05-06-resampling-regularization.pdf`) —
   Resampling and Regularization, for Jul 24
2. Run this week's labs: **3.6** (Linear Regression — `Boston`, `Carseats`) and
   **4.7** (Logistic Regression — `Smarket`; the notebook also covers LDA, QDA,
   naive Bayes, KNN as optional extras)
3. **The threshold question.** On `Default`, pick the threshold you would
   actually recommend to a credit card company. One paragraph defending it.
   [There is no right answer, and I will push back on whatever you choose.]{.hl}
4. Next week: how to *validate* every model you fit today. Cross-validation is
   the tool you will use for the rest of your career.

## Questions? {.center}

::: {.vote}
Every number that looks good is a question, not an answer.

$R^2 = 0.61$. Accuracy $= 97.3\%$.

::: {.fragment .hands style="margin-top:1.2em;"}
See you next Friday
:::
:::

::: notes
If discussion runs dry: "why is logistic regression better here than coding
Yes/No as 0/1 and running least squares?" — a clean check of why we needed the
logistic function at all.

Backup: "the bank fires you if you flag more than 500 customers a month. Now
where is your threshold?" — usually restarts the room.
:::
